metadata.summary: Replace feature commitments with metric movements. Each row: problem, outcome metric (baseline→target), 2-3 candidate solutions. Never fewer than 2 solutions; single-solution rows are features.

content.title: Outcome-Based Roadmaps
content.summary: An outcome-based roadmap replaces feature commitments with measurable metric movements — each row states a problem, an outcome ("metric from baseline to target by horizon"), and 2-3 candidate solutions rather than a single committed feature. The rule: never fewer than 2 candidate solutions per outcome; single-solution rows are feature roadmaps in disguise.

why.p: Feature roadmaps commit to solutions before validating problems, making pivoting politically difficult even when evidence changes. Outcome roadmaps preserve solution flexibility while maintaining strategic alignment — teams stay committed to the goal, not the implementation path. This enables discovery during execution and eliminates the need to renegotiate the roadmap every time a better solution emerges.

list.item: Replacing a feature-list roadmap that has shipped on time but moved no metrics
Communicating priority to engineers who keep asking "why this feature, not that one"
Pre-PMF or post-PMF teams where the problem space is clearer than the solution space
Any team that wants room to discover the right solution without re-renegotiating the roadmap
Hard-deadline contractual obligations (regulatory, partner integration with launch date) — these need a feature roadmap
Pure execution phase of a well-validated initiative — outcome framing adds noise; "ship the redesign" is fine
Teams without metrics infrastructure — you cannot run an outcome roadmap if you cannot measure outcomes
Cultures that punish missed targets — outcome roadmaps require permission to report negative results

section.name: structure
section.p: Defines the required fields for each outcome row and the rule against single-solution commitments.

rule.statement: Every outcome row must contain: problem (1 sentence), outcome_metric (the KPI being moved), baseline (current measured value), target (goal value), horizon (this quarter / next quarter), and candidate_solutions (minimum 2, maximum 5). Rows missing any of these fields are invalid and must be flagged or dropped.
rule.rationale: Incomplete rows silently degrade the roadmap into a feature list. A row with a target but no baseline cannot be tracked. A row with a metric but no horizon is not time-bound and therefore not a commitment.

rule.statement: Require a minimum of 2 candidate solutions per outcome row. A single-solution row is a feature roadmap in disguise — it has already committed to an implementation rather than preserving solution flexibility.
rule.rationale: The entire point of outcome framing is to keep the solution space open while committing to the goal. One solution = already decided. Two or more solutions = the team has permission to discover what actually moves the metric.

rule.statement: Pair every outcome with a kill criterion: "If [leading indicator] does not move by week 6, drop this outcome." The kill criterion is required at commit time, not at quarter end.
rule.rationale: Kill criteria prevent outcome theatre — spending an entire quarter on an outcome that showed no signal in the first six weeks. Without a kill criterion, teams rationalize continuing indefinitely.

example.title: Valid outcome row
example.note: Problem = "Users contact support for questions answered in the product". Outcome_metric = "support tickets / MAU". Baseline = "0.18". Target = "0.12". Horizon = "Q2 2026". Candidate_solutions = ["in-app contextual help tooltips", "searchable FAQ embedded in product", "proactive onboarding email sequence"].

example.title: Feature roadmap disguised as outcome
example.note: Outcome row with single solution: "Reduce churn by building a re-engagement email sequence." Sounds outcome-shaped but has already committed to one solution. Add at least one alternative candidate solution.

example.title: Feature commitment, not outcome
example.note: Feature roadmap row: "Build chat feature." Commits to solution before validating the problem. No metric, no baseline, no flexibility to try a different solution.

section.name: process
section.p: How to convert an existing feature roadmap to outcome rows and generate engineering vs. leadership views from a single source.

rule.statement: For each row in the existing feature roadmap: (1) state the underlying problem in one sentence; (2) restate as an outcome — "[metric] from [baseline] to [target] by [horizon]"; (3) list 2-3 candidate solutions; (4) flag rows where you cannot identify an outcome — these are pet features or infrastructure items.
rule.rationale: Infrastructure and pet features do not have outcomes because their value is not user-facing. Flag them explicitly so stakeholders understand they are funded on a different basis (technical debt, compliance, founder conviction) rather than on outcome impact.

rule.statement: Sort the translated roadmap by outcome impact, not by feature size or team affinity. Impact = magnitude of metric movement multiplied by strategic importance of that metric.
rule.rationale: Feature roadmaps are often sequenced by development order or team availability. Outcome roadmaps must be sequenced by expected impact, which makes trade-offs explicit.

rule.statement: Generate two views from the same source: (a) Engineering view — each outcome with 2-3 candidate solutions, rough effort per option, current confidence; (b) Leadership view — each outcome with metric, baseline, target, current value, confidence, kill criterion. Both views must trace back to identical source rows via a row ID.
rule.rationale: Traceability ID is essential. When a leadership view shows "churn" and the engineering view shows "re-engagement flow," the connection must be explicit. Views that drift from source produce inconsistent commitments.

rule.statement: The customer-facing view stays at problem-statement level only. Never expose internal outcome metrics or implementation details to customers — they ask "when does feature X ship?" and the answer is "we are working on [customer problem], here is what we know about our progress."
rule.rationale: Outcome metrics are internal accountability tools. Exposing churn targets or activation rates to customers creates the wrong conversation and anchors customers to implementation commitments rather than problem resolution.

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Failure modes that produce fake outcome framing or untrackable commitments, with root causes.

antipattern.description: Outcome rewording: translating "Build chat" to "Improve communication outcomes." The words change but no metric, baseline, or target is added. The row is still a feature in disguise.
antipattern.reason: Outcome framing requires a measurable metric movement, not a rephrasing. "Improve communication outcomes" has no baseline and no target, making it untraceable and un-kill-criterion-able.

antipattern.description: Single-solution rows: "Reduce churn from 8% to 5% by building a re-engagement email sequence." One solution means the team has already decided the implementation.
antipattern.reason: The value of outcome framing is preserving solution flexibility. A single committed solution collapses outcome framing back into feature roadmap behavior. Require at least 2 candidate solutions to maintain that flexibility.

antipattern.description: Unbounded horizons: "Improve retention" with no time target. Teams can claim progress at any point without accountability.
antipattern.reason: An outcome without a horizon is not a commitment. "Improve retention to 40% DAU/MAU" is a vibe until "by end of Q2" is added. All outcome rows require explicit horizon specification.

antipattern.description: Hallucinated baselines: outcome rows that list a baseline number that was never measured, because the metric is not yet instrumented.
antipattern.reason: Invented baselines destroy tracking credibility. If the metric is not instrumented, the row must say "TO BE INSTRUMENTED, owner=X" — never an invented number. Teams cannot improve a metric they cannot measure.

antipattern.description: Weekly roadmap rewrites. The agent re-translates the entire roadmap every week, losing the commitment history and making it impossible to track whether outcomes are improving.
antipattern.reason: Outcome roadmaps should be updated at two cadences: (1) weekly metric monitor for progress tracking; (2) quarterly re-decomposition for strategic revision. Rewriting weekly destroys the accountability record.

antipattern.description: Stakeholders silently translating outcome rows back to feature commitments. Leadership hears "reduce support tickets by 30%" and immediately asks "which features will you ship to do that?" and anchors on the answer.
antipattern.reason: This is an ongoing communication problem, not a one-time fix. PMs must actively police the framing in every stakeholder interaction, maintaining a "confirmed solutions" list as a strict subset of the roadmap for external timing commitments.

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: How autonomous agents integrate outcome-roadmap methodologies into product workflows.

A translation agent ingests the existing feature roadmap and rewrites every line as "problem + outcome + candidate solutions" — flagging any item that does not have a clear outcome (those are typically infrastructure or pet features). A presenter agent generates two views: the engineering "options + sprint estimates" view and the leadership "outcome metric + confidence" view. A weekly metric-monitor agent reads dashboards and flags drift. Human PM owns the outcome decomposition, the kill criteria, and the conversation with sales.

Recommended subagents: A roadmap-translator agent (Opus) — converts feature lists to outcome statements; high-leverage strategic step. A view-generator agent (Sonnet) — formats one outcome roadmap into engineering vs leadership vs customer views. A metric-monitor agent — pulls leading-indicator data weekly, flags drift > threshold. faion-mlp-impl-planner-agent — once an outcome wins capacity, plans candidate experiments under it.

Prompt pattern for roadmap translation: "For each row in the current feature roadmap: State the underlying problem (1 sentence). Restate as an outcome: '[metric] from [baseline] to [target] by [horizon]'. List 2-3 candidate solutions (NOT a committed solution). Flag rows where you cannot identify an outcome — these are pet features or infrastructure. Return the rewritten roadmap in markdown, sorted by outcome impact."

Prompt pattern for view generation: "Given outcome roadmap <roadmap.md>, generate two views: 1. Engineering view: each outcome with 2-3 candidate solutions, rough effort per option, current confidence. 2. Leadership view: each outcome with metric, baseline, target, current value, confidence, kill criterion. Both views must trace back to identical source rows."

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Core principles for running outcome-based roadmaps.

rule.statement: Every outcome row has metric + baseline + target + horizon. Drop any row missing those fields.
rule.rationale: Completeness is non-negotiable for tracking and accountability.

rule.statement: Present 2-3 candidate solutions per outcome, never one. Single-solution rows are feature roadmaps in disguise.
rule.rationale: Solution flexibility is the core value proposition.

rule.statement: The customer-facing view stays at problem-statement level; never expose internal outcome metrics.
rule.rationale: Customer conversations are about problems, not internal metrics or timelines.

rule.statement: Keep outcomes time-bounded (this quarter, next quarter). "Improve retention" with no horizon is a vibe, not a roadmap.
Pair every outcome with a kill criterion: "if leading indicator does not move by week 6, drop this outcome."
rule.rationale: Kill criteria prevent indefinite investment in outcomes showing no signal.

rule.statement: Reserve 20% of capacity for "unscheduled outcomes" — discoveries from the field that did not exist when the quarter started.
rule.rationale: Flexibility for emergent opportunities keeps roadmaps responsive.

rule.statement: Sales objection management: maintain a "confirmed solutions" list that is a strict subset of the roadmap; only those get specific timing externally.
rule.rationale: Prevents customers anchoring on one solution within the candidate set.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when LLMs design or execute outcome roadmaps.

antipattern.description: LLMs translate features to outcomes by simply rewording ("Build chat" → "Improve communication outcomes"). That is not an outcome. Force a measurable metric movement per row.
antipattern.reason: Rephrasing is easier than finding metrics. Models lack the domain knowledge to identify outcome-relevant KPIs.

antipattern.description: Agents prefer 1 confident solution per outcome ("the obvious answer"). Force a minimum of 2 candidate solutions to preserve solution discovery.
antipattern.reason: Models optimize for coherent single narratives, not exploring solution space.

antipattern.description: Vague outcome verbs (improve, enhance, optimize) creep in. Add a banned-verb instruction or require numeric movement per outcome.
antipattern.reason: Vague language is natural to language models; specificity requires explicit constraint.

antipattern.description: View-generator agents drift between leadership and engineering views — same row says different things. Require traceability ID per row across views.
antipattern.reason: Models optimize for each-view coherence independently, not cross-view consistency.

antipattern.description: Agents hallucinate baselines. If the metric is not instrumented, the row must say "TO BE INSTRUMENTED, owner=X" — never invented numbers.
antipattern.reason: Plausible numbers are easier to generate than admitting measurement gaps.

antipattern.description: Human-in-loop checkpoint: outcome decomposition (goal → outcomes) is strategic; humans must own the final tree before any view is generated.
antipattern.reason: Strategic decomposition requires human judgment about impact and importance.

antipattern.description: Human-in-loop checkpoint: kill criteria require human sign-off — agents are too quick to delete outcomes the moment metrics wobble.
antipattern.reason: Models over-optimize for "success" signals; kill criteria require human judgment about acceptable risk.

antipattern.description: Do not let the agent re-rewrite the roadmap weekly. Cadence: weekly metric monitor, quarterly re-decomposition.
antipattern.reason: Frequent rewrites destroy accountability and commitment history.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Known failure modes and constraints of outcome-based roadmapping.
section.note: "Reduce churn from 8% to 5%" sounds outcome-shaped but is meaningless without leading indicators that move on a quarterly cadence.
Stakeholders read outcome roadmaps and silently translate them back to features anyway; you have to actively police the framing.
Sales gets frustrated: prospects ask "when does the chat feature ship?" and outcome framing does not answer that question.
Engineering gets frustrated when "options, not committed solution" leaves them unable to plan capacity.
Outcomes without bounds (e.g. "improve activation") become moving targets — must specify "to X by Y date" or it is not an outcome.

template.purpose: Single outcome row schema with all required fields
Python validator: fails roadmap YAML with missing fields or fewer than 2 candidate solutions
